Name: latency_aware_pac_bayes_layer_skipping
Title: Latency-Aware PAC-Bayes Protocols Using Jacobian-Based Layer-Skipping Frameworks
Short Hypothesis: Implementing a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework within a PAC-Bayes context can optimize inference times while maintaining accuracy, offering strict latency guarantees and minimizing overhead.
Related Work: While previous works have explored hardware-level optimizations and specific architectures (e.g., Si-Kintsugi, FPGA-based semantic segmentation), they often fail to integrate theoretical frameworks like PAC-Bayes or focus solely on implementation without a rigorous theoretical backing. This proposal distinguishes itself by combining PAC-Bayes frameworks with a Jacobian-based approach to layer-skipping, thereby providing a novel foundation for latency-aware machine learning that is theoretically grounded and empirically validated.
Abstract: In this proposal, we explore a novel approach to latency-aware machine learning by integrating PAC-Bayes frameworks with a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism. The objective is to establish strict latency guarantees while optimizing the inference process in latency-critical applications. By leveraging spectral stability bounds, we aim to ensure that the performance of machine learning models remains robust against perturbations in input data while minimizing the overhead associated with complex architectures. This study will empirically validate our approach through a series of experiments designed to evaluate throughput-accuracy trade-offs in diverse application settings. We will demonstrate that our proposed framework not only adheres to wall-clock budgets but also outperforms existing methods in both theoretical and practical contexts. The findings will significantly advance the understanding of latency-aware ML systems, providing a pathway for future research in optimization landscapes and information-theoretic constraints.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model with standard inference processing to establish a control for comparison.
2. Develop a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism and integrate it into the baseline model, ensuring strict adherence to latency budgets.
3. Conduct experiments across various datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to evaluate the accuracy and inference time of both the baseline and the modified models.
4. Utilize PAC-Bayes bounds to analyze and quantify the stability of the modified model against perturbations in input data.
5. Measure throughput, accuracy, and latency under different configurations, focusing on the Pareto frontier of performance.
6. Analyze memory bandwidth utilization and perturbation costs to ensure compute-frugal execution.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include overfitting to specific datasets due to the layer-skipping mechanism, which may not generalize well across different domains. Additionally, the complexity of integrating the PAC-Bayes framework with existing architectures may pose implementation challenges. Limitations may also arise from the hardware constraints of testing platforms, which could impact the scalability of findings.
